blocks.content: He closed the sketchbook at some point. He wasn't sure exactly when — it happened the way things happen when you've been somewhere a long time and the decision to leave arrives quietly, without announcement.

The apartment had gone from afternoon to evening while he'd been sitting with it. The light had shifted through all its stages: the gold one, the long-shadow one, the grey one that arrived just before dark committed to itself.

He made dinner. Something simple. He ate standing at the counter, which was something he did when his mind was still working on something and he didn't want to settle into the full ceremony of a table.
He thought: whatever it is, I'm not going anywhere.

He thought it without drama. Without a sense of arrival, as if the decision had been made so long ago — before he knew the question, before he'd understood what was being asked — that arriving at the words was just catching up to something that had already been true.

He washed his plate. He turned off the kitchen light. He went to the window and stood there for a minute the way he sometimes did at the end of a day that had been a lot — looking at the street, the ordinary movement of it, the particular night sounds of the neighborhood doing its unhurried going-home.
Whatever it is. He was not going anywhere.

He would wait until she was ready. He would make it safe. He would show up in the ordinary moments — the coffee, the spring rolls, the texts about breakfast rubrics and thriving plants and things noticed because they were evidence of care. He would be the kind of person she could trust to receive what she wasn't yet ready to give.
He would not ask her to give it before she was ready.
He would just be there. Present, not waiting. There was a difference.
Before he turned off the light, he looked at the sketchbook on the desk. The closed cover. The drawing inside it of a woman at the edge of a porch, looking at something he hadn't tried to draw.
